Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The problem asks us to write a DuckDB SQL query on an event_log table with columns case_id, activity, and timestamp. We need to 1. Identify cases that have the sequence Approve Purchase Requisition Create Order Confirm Order in direct succession no other events in between from the same case. 2. Further filter these cases the time difference between Create Order and Confirm Order must be strictly more than 5 days. 3. Exclude all events from these specific cases from the final result. The result should only contain events from cases that do not meet both the sequence and timing criteria.

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Lets break down step 1 finding cases with the direct succession. We can use window functions. For each event, we can look at the next two activities in the same case. Since the events must be in direct succession, we can use LEAD activity 1 and LEAD activity 2 partitioned by case_id and ordered by timestamp.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: For an event where activity Approve Purchase Requisition next_activity LEAD activity 1 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp next_next_activity LEAD activity 2 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp create_order_ts LEAD timestamp 1 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp confirm_order_ts LEAD timestamp 2 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp.

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: If next_activity Create Order and next_next_activity Confirm Order then the direct succession condition is met. what if a case where Approve Purchase Requisition occurs multiple times The problem says contains the following sequence of three activities in direct succession If any such sequence exists does it filter the entire case out Yes.

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Lets carefully read 1. Identifies any case case_id that contains the following sequence of three activities in direct succession 2. Among those cases that have this specific sequence further refine the selection to only those where the time elapsed between the Create Order event and the Confirm Order event of that sequence is more than 5 days. So a case is excluded if it has AT LEAST ONE occurrence of the sequence where the time between Create Order and Confirm Order is > 5 days.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Writes a CTE that finds all such sequences WITH SequenceMatches AS SELECT case_id activity LEAD activity 1 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_activity LEAD activity 2 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_next_activity LEAD timestamp 1 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as create_order_ts LEAD timestamp 2 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as confirm_order_ts FROM event_log.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: is DATEDIFF standard in DuckDB DuckDB supports DATEDIFF day start end Lets use next_next_ts - next_ts > INTERVAL 5 DAY Since its DuckDB interval arithmetic works timestamp - timestamp > INTERVAL 5 DAY.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Does DATEDIFF day create_order_ts confirm_order_ts > 5 mean the same as confirm_order_ts - create_order_ts > INTERVAL 5 DAY DATEDIFF day 2023-01-01 00:00:00 2023-01-06 00:00:00 returns 5 So DATEDIFF > 5 means at least 6 full days Using interval comparison correctly checks if the exact duration exceeds 5 days.

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - PE
Text: What if a case has Approve Purchase Requisition Create Order Approve Purchase Requisition Confirm Order the first Approve has next Create Order next_next Approve does not match the second Approve has next Confirm does not match. What if Approve Purchase Requisition Create Order Some other Confirm Order next_activity Create Order next_next Some other does not match.

Name: Validation - IND
Text: What if there are multiple events with the same timestamp in the same case Ordering by timestamp is the best we can do and standard for such process mining SQL queries.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The query logic is perfectly aligned with the prompt requirements. Final query seems solid.
